passages.explanation: Jesus enters the synagogue again, and a man with a shrivelled hand is there. They watch Jesus closely to see if he'll heal on the Sabbath, so they can accuse him. Jesus tells the man with the shrivelled hand: stand up in front of everyone. Then he asks them: which is lawful on the Sabbath — to do good or to do evil, to save life or to kill? They remain silent. He looks around at them in anger, deeply distressed at their stubborn hearts. He tells the man: stretch out your hand. He stretches it out, and his hand is completely restored. Then the Pharisees go out and immediately begin plotting with the Herodians how to kill Jesus. This is only chapter 3 and they're already planning his death. Mark records both Jesus' anger and his grief — he's not emotionless about their resistance. The Pharisees and Herodians are natural enemies (religious purists and political collaborators), but Jesus unites them against him.

Jesus withdraws with his disciples to the lake. A large crowd from Galilee follows. When they hear about everything he's doing, many people come from Judea, Jerusalem, Idumea, the regions across the Jordan, and around Tyre and Sidon. Because of the crowd, he tells his disciples to have a small boat ready so the people won't crush him. He's healed many, so those with diseases push forward to touch him. Whenever the impure spirits see him, they fall down before him and shout: you are the Son of God! But he gives them strict orders not to tell anyone who he is. The crowds are becoming dangerous — physically crushing. And the demons keep trying to out him publicly. Jesus consistently silences them. He controls when and how his identity is revealed, because a premature revelation would trigger the wrong kind of response.

Jesus goes up on a mountainside and calls to him those he wants, and they come. He appoints twelve that they might be with him and that he might send them out to preach and have authority to drive out demons. These are the twelve he appoints: Simon (to whom he gives the name Peter), James son of Zebedee and his brother John (to whom he gives the name Boanerges, which means 'sons of thunder'), Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas Iscariot, who betrayed him. Mark's description of the Twelve's purpose is two-fold: to be with him (relationship) and to be sent out (mission). Being with Jesus comes first. The nicknames — Rock, Sons of Thunder — suggest Jesus sees what they'll become, not just what they are.

Jesus enters a house, and again a crowd gathers so that he and his disciples can't even eat. When his family hears about this, they go to take charge of him, because they say: he's out of his mind. The teachers of the law who come from Jerusalem say: he's possessed by Beelzebul! By the prince of demons he's driving out demons. Jesus calls them over and speaks in parables: how can Satan drive out Satan? If a kingdom is divided against itself, it can't stand. If a house is divided against itself, it can't stand. And if Satan opposes himself and is divided, he can't stand — his end has come. No one can enter a strong man's house without first tying up the strong man. Then he can plunder his house. Truly I tell you, people can be forgiven all their sins and every slander they utter, but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven — they are guilty of an eternal sin. He says this because they're saying he has an impure spirit. Jesus' family thinks he's lost his mind. The religious experts think he's demon-possessed. Both are wrong, but the scribes' accusation is worse — they're calling the Holy Spirit's work demonic. That's the unforgivable sin: not a one-time slip, but a settled conviction that calls good evil.

Jesus' mother and brothers arrive. Standing outside, they send someone in to call him. A crowd is sitting around him, and they tell him: your mother and brothers are outside looking for you. He asks: who are my mother and brothers? Then he looks at those seated in a circle around him: here are my mother and brothers! Whoever does God's will is my brother and sister and mother. Jesus doesn't go outside. He redefines family around obedience to God rather than blood. This would have been shocking in a culture where family loyalty was the highest social obligation. Jesus isn't rejecting his family — he's expanding the category to include everyone in the room.
